Given the context about firearm injuries and the focus on bullet-related injuries, "bullet fingerprinting" would logically refer to primary markings made during the discharge process that could help identify individual firearms or rounds fired. Therefore, considering the available options and focusing on what marks are created initially upon firing, we conclude that "primary marking" best fits the description given the context of firearm injuries and ballistics.
